coredata.dc:title: Green simple preparation of LiNiO2 nanopowder for lithium ion battery
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Taha, T.A.
El-Molla, M.M.
coredata.dc:description: This work aims at preparing LiNiO2 nanopowder using egg white as a chelating agent. Several experimental measurements applied to the samples like X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and FTIR spectroscopy. Analysis by XRD revealed crystalline particles of approximately 19−57 nm in diameter with hexagonal crystal structure. The lattice parameters (a and c) show a small change with calcination temperature and the lattice distortion c/a slightly decreased. The overall morphology of the LiNiO2 crystalline powder investigated by SEM and TEM, which indicated nanopowder with fine structure. The BET surface area of the synthesized crystalline powder was measured to be 11.7 m2/g, 9.07 m2/g, 8.79 m2/g, and 8.53 m2/g at calcination temperatures 0, 500, 600 and 700 °C, respectively. The FTIR spectra exhibit vibrational modes correlated with the vibrations of NiO6 and LiO6 octahedral units in the region 400 - 700 cm−1. Moreover, FTIR spectra for all the samples show peaks at 860 and 1430 cm−1 associated with Ni–O bond.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
LiNiO2,nanopowder
Lithium ion battery

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TGA-DTG scans of the LiNiO2 as prepared powder.
(a) SEM micrograph and (b) EDX spectrum of the LiNiO2 sample calcinated at 700 °C for 10 h.
TEM image of the LiNiO2 sample calcinated at 700 °C for 10 h.
BET plots of LiNiO2 using points collected at the pressure range 0.05 to 0.3.
FTIR spectra of the LiNiO2 nanopowder.
XRD crystallite size and lattice parameters of LiNiO2 nanopowder.

head.ce:title: Green simple preparation of LiNiO2 nanopowder for lithium ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, College of Science and Arts, Jouf University, P.O. Box 756, Al-Gurayyat, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, College of Science and Arts, Jouf University, P.O. Box 756, Al-Gurayyat, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work aims at preparing LiNiO2 nanopowder using egg white as a chelating agent. Several experimental measurements applied to the samples like X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and FTIR spectroscopy. Analysis by XRD revealed crystalline particles of approximately 19−57 nm in diameter with hexagonal crystal structure. The lattice parameters (a and c) show a small change with calcination temperature and the lattice distortion c/a slightly decreased. The overall morphology of the LiNiO2 crystalline powder investigated by SEM and TEM, which indicated nanopowder with fine structure. The BET surface area of the synthesized crystalline powder was measured to be 11.7 m2/g, 9.07 m2/g, 8.79 m2/g, and 8.53 m2/g at calcination temperatures 0, 500, 600 and 700 °C, respectively. The FTIR spectra exhibit vibrational modes correlated with the vibrations of NiO6 and LiO6 octahedral units in the region 400 - 700 cm−1. Moreover, FTIR spectra for all the samples show peaks at 860 and 1430 cm−1 associated with Ni–O bond.

ce:section.ce:para: More recent studies have confirmed that nanomaterials possess both high storage capacity and mechanical stability for long term cycling [1–8]. Its great advantages are that it accounts for the small particle size that enhanced rate capability through reduction of electron diffusion, the high surface area also could make lithium absorption more favorable for high capacities; the particle size may also better accommodate the crystalline expansion during lithiation; and many nanomaterials can offer enhanced mechanical support and electrical percolation within a network [9–12].

Several studies have revealed that LiMO2 (M = Ni or Co) nanomaterials are of great importance as cathode materials in rechargeable Li-ion batteries [13–18]. Extensive research has shown that LiNiO2 is one of the most significant cathode electrode materials because of it is high discharge capacity, high operating voltage, high energy density, cost effective and echo-friendly when compared with LiCoO2 material [19–21].To date there are great attention on the preparation of LiNiO2 with different synthetic methods to obtain high purity nanopowder [22–25]. It has previously been observed that LiNiO2 prepared in air minimize the cost in contrast to O2 atmosphere [26–28]. Solid-state reaction, sol–gel co-precipitation, ultrasonic spray pyrolysis and emulsion methods applied to the LiNiO2 nanopowder synthesis [29–34]. However, to the best of authors knowledge, no report has been found so far using egg white as a chelating agent for synthesis LiNiO2 nanopowder.

The recent research effort that has been put into LiNiO2 nanopowder for Li ion battery could be described in terms of the following directions: (1) preparation of LiNiO2 nanopowder by low cost and environmentally benign method, (2) structural characterization of the prepared samples via XRD, SEM, TEM, Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) surface area and FTIR measurements.

Fine particles of LiNiO2 nanopowder were prepared by using, analar grades of lithium nitrate and nickel nitrate which weighed and dissolved in 1:1 egg white and distilled water solution. The egg white enhanced the the reaction rate and combine Li and nickel ions to produce LiNiO2 nanoparticles. The solutions mixed for 2 h via mechanical stirrer at room temperature. The above solution was dried at 100 °C overnight and then burnt at 200 °C. The obtained gel grinded and calcinated at 500, 600 and 700 °C for 10 h with a heating rate (5 °C/min) and shall be referred to as LN0, LN1, LN2 and LN3, respectively.
TGA instrument is TG-DTA (SDT Q600 V20.9) with the alumina plate as the sample holder. The operating range is between RT and 1000 °C in N2 atmosphere with a temperature increase rate of 10 °C/min.

XRD patterns in this work are obtained on the Bruker, AXS D8 Advance diffractometer, which worked at 40 KV and 30 mA with a monochromatized Cu Kα beam (where λ is equal to 0.15406 nm). The morphologies and elemental analysis of nanomaterials are performed on the Quanta FEG 250 field emission scanning electron microscope.

TEM measurements are performed on the JEOL-200CX transmission electron microscope with a tapping mode. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller surface area was estimated from experimental data on a Quanta chrome instrument at a relative pressure of P/P0 = 0.05 – 0.25. The FTIR spectra of LiNiO2 nanopowder are obtained by Jasco 6100 FTIR spectrometer.

TGA-DTG data for the as-prepared powder displayed in Fig. 1 . These results revealed exothermic reactions under 400 °C. The exothermic peak around 65 °C is due to the ion exchange reaction [35]. Additionally, the exothermic peak around 263 °C comes due to nitrates decomposition this is may be reduction of lithium nitrate and nickel nitrate via egg white complex agent [29,36].

Fig. 2 represents the XRD patterns for the prepared LiNiO2 nanopowder at different calcination temperatures. For the resulting plot, diffraction lines correlated with hexagonal crystal structure of LiNiO2 as confirmed by (JCPDS No. 23-0362). One finds diffraction peaks indexed with Li2CO3 for the samples calcinated at 500 and 600 °C as well as the prepared sample [37]. Heat-treatment of the samples at higher temperatures gives slightly sharp diffraction peaks. The diffraction peak (003) has very low intensity at the calcination temperature 700 °C which indicates tranformation to cubic crystal structure [38]. The purity and crystalinity of LiNiO2 nanopowder could be enhanced with calcination in oxygen atmosphere and or at temperature higher than 700 °C [39,40].

The average crystallite size of LiNiO2 was estimated via Scherrer formula (D = 0.9λ/βcosθ), λ refers to X-ray wavelength, β is called peak breadth, [41–52] and W-H plot method (the calculations completed by XPowder software) [53,54]. The crystal sizes summarized in Table 1 increased with increasing the calcination temperature.

The distance, d, between adjacent planes in the set (hkl) for a crystal in the hexagonal is (
1
d 2
=
4 3
h 2
+ h k +
k 2
a 2
+
l 2
c 2
) [55]. In Table 1, the lattice parameters (a and c) show a small change with calcination temperature and the lattice distortion c/a slightly decreased. The finding from the present study agrees relatively well with that reported in the literature [29,36].

Fig. 3 a illustrates the FE-SEM micrograph of the LiNiO2 nanopowder calcinated at 700 °C for 10 h. We remarked the fine structure of the prepared nanopowder with particle size less than 1 μm, which is in good agreement with the results of the past studies [37,39].

The expected elements are depicted from EDX spectrum of the LiNiO2 nanopowder as shown in Fig. 3b. The atomic percentage of Ni and O are 18.51 and 47.31%. High-resolution TEM image of LiNiO2 nanopowder calcinated at 700 °C for 10 h is presented in Fig. 4 . It is observed that the prepared nanopowder are nearly hexagonal structures which in good agreement with the XRD results.

The corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern for the LiNiO2 nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 4.b, revealing the crystal lattice parameter of the LiNiO2 nanoparticles, in accord with the hexagonal phase of LiNiO2.

The specific surface area was measured by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method (Fig. 5 ). The specific BET surface area of the LiNiO2 nanopowder calcinated at 0, 500, 600 and 700 °C are 11.7 m2/g, 9.07 m2/g, 8.79 m2/g, and 8.53 m2/g, respectively. The large specific large surface can be beneficial for the lithium intercalation and de-intercalation, leading to the improvement of cycling performance and the rate capability.
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra can be used to determine crystalline structure and perfection of LiNiO2 nanopowder.

The measured FTIR spectra for the LiNiO2 nanopowder are shown in Fig. 6 . The FTIR spectra exhibit vibrational modes correlated with the vibrations of NiO6 and LiO6 octahedral units in the region 400 - 700 cm−1 [37]. Thus, the peak around 432 cm−1 is due to the asymmetric stretching vibration of Li – O in LiO6 and the bending vibrations of NiO6, i.e., ν[(Ni – O – Li)], appeared at 627 and 687 cm−1 [56,57]. Moreover, FTIR spectra for all the samples show peaks at 860 and 1430 cm−1 associated with Ni–O bond [38]. The absorption bands observed in the 2500 – 3600 cm−1 range are due to the OH groups [41].

This study illustrates the preparation of LiNiO2 nanopowder using egg white as a chelating agent for lithium ion battery. Based on XRD measurements, the LiNiO2 nanopowder has hexagonal crystal structure and crystallite size 21.69–57.05 nm as estimated from WH method. The lattice parameters (a and c) show a small change with calcination temperature and the lattice distortion c/a slightly decreased. The overall morphology of the LiNiO2 crystalline powder investigated by SEM and TEM, which indicated nanopowder with fine structure. The BET surface area of the synthesized crystalline powder was measured to be 11.7 m2/g, 9.07 m2/g, 8.79 m2/g, and 8.53 m2/g at calcination temperatures 0, 500, 600 and 700 °C, respectively. The FTIR spectra exhibit vibrational modes correlated with the vibrations of NiO6 and LiO6 octahedral units in the region 400 - 700 cm−1. Future work on the prepared LiNiO2 nanopowder will involve another experimental technique to check the electrochemical properties.
The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors of this article gratefully acknowledge the Jouf University, Sakaka, Saudi Arabia, for financially support this work (project number 40/117).
